title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Ten: In which Calcifer promises Sophie a hint (Fragment 3)
text:
“I envy you being able to walk all that way so easily,” Sophie said, sewing a blue triangle to silver braid. “How was Ma—my niece?”

Michael gladly left the workbench and sat on the stool by the hearth to tell her all about his day. Then he asked about Sophie’s. The result was that when Howl shouldered the door open with his arms full of parcels, Michael was not even looking busy. He was rolling around on the stool laughing at the duel spell.

Howl backed into the door to shut it and leaned there in a tragic attitude. “Look at you all!” he said. “Ruin stares me in the face. I slave all day for you all. And not one of you, even Calcifer, can spare time to say hello!”

Michael sprang up guiltily and Calcifer said, “I never do say hello.”

“Is something wrong?” asked Sophie.

“That’s better,” said Howl. “Some of you are pretending to notice me at last. How kind of you to ask, Sophie. Yes, something is wrong. The King has asked me officially to find his brother for him—with a strong hint that destroying the Witch of the Waste would come in handy too—and you all sit there and laugh!”

By now it was clear that Howl was in a mood to produce green slime any second. Sophie hurriedly put her sewing away. “I’ll make some hot buttered toast,” she said.

“Is that all you can do in the face of tragedy?” Howl asked. “Make toast! No, don’t get up. I’ve trudged here laden with stuff for you, so the least you can do is show polite interest. Here.” He tipped a shower of parcels into Sophie’s lap and handed another to Michael.

Mystified, Sophie unwrapped things: several pairs of silk stockings; two parcels of the finests cambric petticoats, with flounces, lace, and satin insets; a pair of elastic-sided boots in dove-gray suede; a lace shawl; and a dress of gray watered silk trimmed with lace that matched the shawl. Sophie took one professional look at each and gasped. The lace alone was worth a fortune. She stroked the silk of the dress, awed.

Michael unwrapped a handsome new velvet suit. “You must have spent every bit that was in the silk purse!” he said ungratefully. “I don’t need this. You’re the one who needs a new suit.”

Howl hooked his boot into what remained of the blue-and-silver suit and held it up ruefully. Sophie had been working hard, but it was still more hole than suit. “How selfless I am,” he said. “But I can’t send you and Sophie to blacken my name to the King in rags. The King would think I didn’t look after my old mother properly. Well, Sophie? Are the boots the right size?”

Sophie looked up from her awed stroking. “Are you being kind,” she said, “or cowardly? Thank you very much and no I won’t.”

“What ingratitude!” Howl exclaimed, spreading out both arms. “Let’s have green slime again! After which I shall be forced to move the castle a thousand miles away and never see my lovely Lettie again!”

Michael looked at Sophie imploringly. Sophie glowered. She saw well enough that the happiness of both her sisters depended on her agreeing to see the King. With green slime in reserve. “You haven’t asked me to do anything yet,” she said. “You’ve just said I’m going to.”

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Ten: In which Calcifer promises Sophie a hint

vocabulary.definition: Filled with respect, wonder, or amazement.
vocabulary.example: Sophie took one professional look at each and gasped, awed.

vocabulary.definition: To damage someone's reputation.
vocabulary.example: I can’t send you to blacken my name to the King in rags.

vocabulary.definition: A fine, white cotton fabric used in clothing or handkerchiefs.
vocabulary.example: Parcels of the finest cambric petticoats.

vocabulary.definition: The act of causing damage so severe that something no longer exists or works.
vocabulary.example: A strong hint that destroying the Witch of the Waste would come in handy too.

vocabulary.definition: A magical contest fought between two individuals using spells.
vocabulary.example: Michael was rolling around on the stool laughing at the duel spell.

vocabulary.definition: Boots that have elastic panels on the sides for easy wearing.
vocabulary.example: A pair of elastic-sided boots in dove-gray suede.

vocabulary.definition: A gathered or pleated strip of fabric added as a decorative feature on a garment.
vocabulary.example: Petticoats with flounces and lace.

vocabulary.definition: To look angry or sullen, especially by staring intensely.
vocabulary.example: Sophie glowered.

vocabulary.definition: The floor of a fireplace, often extending into a room, symbolising home or warmth.
vocabulary.example: Michael sat on the stool by the hearth to tell her all about his day.

vocabulary.definition: A slight or indirect indication or suggestion.
vocabulary.example: The King has asked me officially to find his brother for him—with a strong hint that destroying the Witch of the Waste would come in handy too.

vocabulary.definition: Doing something quickly because of being late or an urgent situation.
vocabulary.example: Sophie hurriedly put her sewing away.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that asks for something desperately or earnestly.
vocabulary.example: Michael looked at Sophie imploringly.

vocabulary.definition: The lack of thankfulness or appreciation.
vocabulary.example: What ingratitude! exclaimed Howl.

vocabulary.definition: Heavily loaded or weighed down with a load.
vocabulary.example: I’ve trudged here laden with stuff for you.

vocabulary.definition: A temporary state of mind or feeling.
vocabulary.example: Howl was in a mood to produce green slime any second.

vocabulary.definition: In a formal or public way, authorised by someone in authority.
vocabulary.example: The King has asked me officially to find his brother for him.

vocabulary.definition: A package or bundle of things wrapped up together.
vocabulary.example: He tipped a shower of parcels into Sophie’s lap.

vocabulary.definition: A woman’s undergarment hanging from the waist, worn beneath a skirt or dress.
vocabulary.example: Finest cambric petticoats with flounces and lace.

vocabulary.definition: To behave as if something is true when it is not.
vocabulary.example: Some of you are pretending to notice me at last.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that expresses sorrow or regret, especially in a slightly humorous manner.
vocabulary.example: Howl held up the suit ruefully.

vocabulary.definition: Having little or no concern for oneself, devoted to the needs of others.
vocabulary.example: How selfless I am, he said.

vocabulary.definition: The activity of joining or attaching fabrics using needle and thread.
vocabulary.example: Sophie said, sewing a blue triangle to silver braid.

vocabulary.definition: To have enough free time available.
vocabulary.example: Not one of you, even Calcifer, can spare time to say hello!

vocabulary.definition: A type of leather with a soft, fuzzy surface made from the underside of animal skin.
vocabulary.example: Boots in dove-gray suede.

vocabulary.definition: A very sad or terrible event or situation.
vocabulary.example: Is that all you can do in the face of tragedy?

vocabulary.definition: To walk slowly and with heavy steps, typically because of exhaustion or harsh conditions.
vocabulary.example: I’ve trudged here laden with stuff for you.
